review: The casual system is broken. The queue time is over 30 minutes or even hours if I only want to play specific maps. When I join the server, the match has already ended before I even attempt to choose a class to play. Furthermore, there will always be one team that gets only half the number of players as the other team. This causes stomps frequently (not fun at all). And no, joining community servers doesn't help. Because of my region, I can't play on most popular servers due to high latency. Valve needs to fix these issues right now. And for God's sake, stop opening cases. Stop gambling. Stop giving your money to Valve. Stop pretending the state of the game is completely fine. We need to pressure Valve. TF2 deserves better treatment. It being an almost 20-year-old game is not an excuse to ignore all the problems. I just want to play the game normally. #bringbackquickplay

I couldn't bear this any longer than 20 minutes, and uninstalled the game on a 3rd or 4th level; and I had enough of patience to play through another Valve masterpiece - Half-Life 2, with it's sections where NPCs speak for minutes, Alyx "jokes", or anything related to that painfully cringy "dog"-thing. Game is just a work of art. They took Doom 2016's torch and ran with it, basically turning it into a 3D platformer in the best possible way. It's fast paced as hell, with utterly awesome sound, visuals, and level design. There's a wide range of gameplay, while still making you use every weapon at your disposable. It's not a casual shooter. Even on the easiest difficulty it can be pretty difficult for a casual player, and the DLC turns it up to 11. Either way, basically it's one of the best shooters I've ever played second only to Half Life 1. Well worth it at full price. If it wasn't 90GB I would keep it installed at all times just for the occasional demon slaying, it's just a bloody delight.

This has been one of the best story games I've ever played. Definitely in my top 3 games played of all time. First the graphics are absolutely spectacular even for a game this old. The cities look gorgeous and actually feel alive unlike some other games. Once I got to Novigrad I was absolutely stunned by the buildings and scenery. The story was great with no dull moments. The first few hours were probably the worst because I didn't really have any idea what was going on. The characters are some of the most depth I've seen and all have their own sense of humour and personality. Geralt was really fun to play and his dry sense of humour made me laugh a handful of times. The combat is great and the dismemberment was amazing. However, for me it was a little too easy. I never really struggled on any bosses and they never really look me more than one or two tries to beat. Will take a small break and start playing the DLCs since I've been told they are even better than base game.

I walk through the evening rain, Collecting scars instead of gold or fame. Monster inside people, which should be tamed. No one is innocent in this game. Pour me a mug beside the dwarven fire, Where laughter climbs much higher than the pyre. An elf will sing beneath the silver moon, While humans start another pointless feud by noon. I swear I wasn't born with yellow eyes, Yet every crowded street feels like disguise. A migrant of my own strange, noisy race, Forever looking for a quieter place. For every sunset paints the world anew, And every road still whispers, "Walk it through." If life is but a contract, signed in pain, I'd choose the Path... and choose it all again.

Very good game. The story is good, the gameplay is too, and you get extreme freedom in your approach to pretty much everything. For example, I made a pact with a devil to obtain a powerful weapon meant to be used against the final boss. But since I had no intention of honoring the pact, I opened a portal to the devil's hellish manor, stole the contract, looted his private version of a British colonial museum, took out his pleasure slave, and freed his most important prisoner. When the devil tried to intercept me at the portal, I threw a backpack with four nuclear explosives (which I had stolen from a terrorist organization of gnomes) right in his face, blowing him and his minions to pieces. Definitely a highly recommendable game with massive potential for multiple playthroughs due to the nearly unlimited possible playstyles."

Portal 2 is easily one of the best puzzle games I've ever played. The puzzles are incredibly well designed and always feel rewarding to solve. I especially loved the glitch mechanics and all the creative ways the game lets you think outside the box. The gameplay, story, and characters come together perfectly, making this a game that's hard to put down. Even years after its release, it still feels fresh and unique. I wanted to like this game, but it wasn't for me. My first impression was that the tutorial dragged on far too long. Nearly 80 minutes into the game, I was still being sent on resource-gathering errands and building a base instead of feeling like I was exploring the universe. A poor starting planet didn't help, as much of my time was spent walking around looking for basic materials rather than doing anything exciting. The controls and camera also felt awkward to me, and I never reached a point where movement or exploration felt comfortable. While I understand the game opens up later, I don't think a player should have to spend several hours waiting for the fun to begin. Not a bad game—just one whose opening hours failed to hook me.

The essence of the game is the experience of living as an outlaw. You are constantly making choices to take care of your crew, who are basically your only family in a world they see as pretty brutal. Rockstar completely nailed the immersion here, there are so many tiny, complex mechanics that just hook you and make you feel like you're actually living the life you were promised. But honestly, the real heart of the game is Arthur’s journey. It's all about him realizing there's a different way to look at life, and that it’s literally never too late to turn things around, do the right thing, and actually make a difference.

If you like constantly fast paced, then this is not for you. Traveling takes time, gathering resources takes time. But unlike some games where it would be more fitting to make it instant, it fits in here. Unless you have friends playing with you, it's a game about being a space nomad if you want it to be, picking up odd jobs for money and resources, and flying through space. Certainly a game for very specific types of people.

God, I hate to make this review, but I do not recommend the game in its current state. Don't get me wrong, this game has some of the best mechanics and balance from a team shooter I've seen, if not the best, but the current state of the casual matchmaker leaves a lot to be desired. Games end up being total domination or a complete loss, which means you don't really get to enjoy the game either way. Only the grindier maps consistently offer actual matches to the stalemate-y nature of them... and then you are playing in a grindy chokepoint rich environment which just means infinite projectile spam. Since competitive is nonexistent, the only place you can really actually go and play are community servers. It is a shame, because this game can be really good. Really really good, some of the best fun I've had gaming ever, but the magic sort of fades after searching for a balanced match for 15 minutes at a time.

10/10 I have watched plenty of DnD content, and I've always wanted to try it. Like many others I simply don't know anyone to play with. This game scratched that itch perfectly. This is probably the best RPG I've ever played. Between the incredible acting, beautiful environments, and incredible story we also see freedom. The freedom this game gives the players is beyond anything I've ever seen from any game. Don't want to fight this guy? Alright, you can probably just turn invisible and walk away. Boss too hard? Turn into a giant owl bear and bodyslam him into oblivion. The devs SHOWED UP with this game. Truly a remarkable once in a lifetime experience. And thing is, each playthrough is so unique you practically have infinite stories to experience if you so choose. You can also play with others if you so choose which makes it a great game to bond over. Truly fantastic work and one of the only games I've ever gladly paid full price for.

The one day I get injured and have to stay home is also the only day my friends and I can actually play together because of our time zone differences. So I buy this game to play with them... and instead we spend hours troubleshooting a version mismatch that exists because the Windows and Mac Steam versions aren't even on the same build. Seriously, what did I pay for? A multiplayer game where I can't play with my friends because the developers can't keep supported platforms on the same version? There is no workaround, no rollback, no manual update—just "wait." If you're going to sell the game on both Windows and Mac through Steam, keep the versions synchronized. Otherwise, don't advertise it as a multiplayer experience across supported platforms. Completely wasted my time and money. Extremely disappointing.

The primary achievement of Elden Ring is the seamless integration of the signature hardcore Souls gameplay within an open-world framework. Exploration: The absence of excessive map markers ensures that exploration is driven solely by player curiosity. Combat: An immense diversity of builds—ranging from magic to various melee and ranged options—allows for complete stylistic freedom. Difficulty: Rather than being merely oppressive, the difficulty serves to motivate the player to study enemy timings and patterns. This stands as FromSoftware’s finest work to date.
The combat mechanics are what make Sekiro so special. Instead of just dodging and attacking, the game pushes you to deflect, stay aggressive, and break your enemy's posture.

The theming was so wonderful. Extremely few bugs, performance was shockingly good for the quality of the world around as well as my computer's relatively low specs. This game is great, a huge shame that TC learned all the wrong lessons from this game for SS. It's certainly not for everyone. The biggest stumbling block for most people is runbacks, which are not my personal favorite but the game is easy enough that it didn't ruin the experience for me. Nice and chill. Mechanics are simple and the gameplay loop is kind of whatever you want it to be. Gameplay loop at its core revolves arounds gathering minerals from different planets and traveling between systems inhabited by either small, trade oriented lizard merchants who do not seem entirely trustworthy, orcs with a very warrior-like mentality who sound like they might invade a neighbour if given a chance or clankers. There looks to be a 4th race but I'm not at the station to socialize so read a wiki or something. Expeditions bring a new twist typically revolving around the latest update, some have been great, others not so much. A bit of variety is always welcome regardless. Most important part is that the game has taken galactic steps forward from the abomination it used to be. Try it out, at this point the content is so varied there is bound to be something there for almost anyone. This game feels more like a PC hardware benchmark or a technology showcase than an actual game. I had to spend a lot of time tweaking the graphics settings just to get it running smoothly. After the main story, the open world feels incredibly empty. The NPCs are lifeless, and while the environment is visually stunning, there's almost nothing that makes exploring worthwhile. The world looks beautiful, but it lacks the depth, interactivity, and sense of discovery found in games like The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt or the Fallout. Those games constantly reward exploration with memorable quests, interesting characters, and meaningful encounters, while this world rarely gives you a reason to leave the main path. Overall, it feels more like a linear, story-driven game disguised as an open-world experience than a true open-world RPG. I'll give it another try if the Ultimate Edition gets a much deeper discount. At its current price, I don't think it's worth recommending.

CDPR creates compelling and thought-provoking worlds, and Cyberpunk 2077 is no exception. Night City is a dystopian retro-futuristic world that feels believable, dense, and alive. There’s a ton of interesting side content that keeps you engaged for a long time. It’s one of the few games where questing never really feels like a chore encounters are varied, well written, worldbuilding of Night City is truly something special. My only real criticism is that combat areas can sometimes feel a bit repetitive or unimaginative. It’s a shame, because the architecture and environmental design in the game are outstanding and could have been used in even more interesting ways.

Overall 9/10 I just have beef with one of the endings, but other than that, this game is an absolute masterpiece. Soundtrack is great, quests are fun, weapon options are vast, exploring is rewarding, driving around is entertaining. Great replay value. If you dropped this game due to initial release issues, it's time to give it another try. You will NOT be disappointed.

I had been looking forward to playing this game after watching countless videos about it. The world, lore, and overall concept are exactly the kind of RPG I enjoy. Unfortunately, I couldn't get past the PC controls. I don't mind difficult games, Elden Ring's tough enemies made the world feel more immersive. What made the experience frustrating was the keyboard and mouse implementation. Many key bindings feel unintuitive and clunky. Difficulty should come from the game's mechanics and enemies, not from fighting the controls. Good quality-of-life features and intuitive controls don't make a game easier; they simply make it more enjoyable to play. It's clear that Elden Ring was designed primarily with a controller in mind. I may revisit the game once I get a controller, because everything else about it genuinely appeals to me. But for now, the PC controls prevent me from enjoying what otherwise seems like an incredible game.

I still dont care for the bosses much, but such a cute game makes it a lovely place to share with someone you care about, building a cute world to make memories together hunting for drops or building cute buildings and outfits or spamming fish quests for hours on end. One additional note: avoid Rule34. I genuinely can't believe I got a major spoiler from it.

"Would you recommend this game?" That's the question Steam asks at the end of every review. For Stardew Valley, my answer will always be yes. This is my go-to recommendation for just about anyone, regardless of what kinds of games they usually play. Sure, it's a farming game at its core, but that's only part of what makes it great. You can fight monsters in the mines, build friendships (or romance), discover magic, decorate your farm, fish, forage, and even play with friends in co-op. It's one of those rare games that's easy to pick up, hard to put down, and somehow always manages to pull me back in. Simply put, I love this game, and I can't recommend it enough.

Elden Ring is one of the best Soulslike games I've ever played. It offers stunning visuals, an incredible world, addictive exploration, deep lore, memorable characters, excellent gameplay, diverse enemies, and some of the best boss fights in gaming. I was initially worried that an open-world Souls game might lose what made the series special, but FromSoftware proved the opposite. The open world is one of the game's biggest strengths. My only real criticism is that the game relies too much on external guides. Without YouTube, Reddit, or the Elden Ring Wiki, you'll likely miss important mechanics, hidden areas, quests, and even struggle to create an effective build. The downside is that researching these things can easily lead to story spoilers. Despite that, I loved every minute of the game. The first few hours were challenging, but once my build came together, I couldn't put it down. I enjoyed it so much that I completed it 100%. Highly recommended.

One of the best games I've ever played in my life. It can't really be considered a souls-like or a hack and slash, because honestly, it's neither, this is a uniquely different kind of game unlike anything I've seen before. I won't lie to you: it's genuinely challenging, and it demands that you master its mechanics, otherwise you simply won't be able to progress through the story. I'd even go as far as calling it a rhythm game, because rather than dodging attacks (which you can do, but it's not recommended), its core strength lies in parrying. You have to block with precision, matching the rhythmic patterns of your enemies, that's the most effective way to defeat them. The story isn't all that amazing and don't expect any major plot twists, but it's still a step above average. Once again, the game truly shines through its incredible gameplay, which will keep you hooked. Highly recommended.

I played *The Witcher 3* after finishing *Red Dead Redemption 2*, and I think that comparison affected how much I enjoyed it. The character movement and horse-riding mechanics felt significantly less fluid and polished than in RDR2. I only played for a little over six hours, but the game never fully immersed me. Many systems and concepts were introduced very quickly, without enough explanation. For example, I was introduced to several magical signs at once, but I did not feel that the game properly taught me when or how to use them. I also found the crafting system confusing and had little idea how it worked. The game presented me with a large number of dialogue choices, but it was rarely clear whether they would meaningfully affect the story. Because of this, many of the choices felt unnecessary rather than important. I was also frustrated when some quests failed without an obvious way to retry them. Finally, the motion blur gave me headaches and made the game uncomfortable to play.

The honest answer is not necessarily. RDR2 features a slow narrative pace and highly realistic mechanics (such as maintaining weapons, feeding your horse, and unskippable looting animations). If you are looking for fast-paced, instant-gratification action, the game might feel tedious at first. However, if you are a player who loves enjoying the journey, exploring the world, and appreciating deep storytelling, this will easily become one of the greatest experiences of your life. The Verdict: Red Dead Redemption 2 is a love letter to the Wild West. It is a melancholy adventure that is beautiful, brutal, and unforgettable. When the credits finally roll, you won’t just feel like you finished a game—you’ll feel like you’ve lost a friend. A must-play at least once in your life.

It had a shaky start, but after a few updates and an incredible DLC, this has become one of the best rpgs ever made. The characters, writing, and worldbuilding are excellent, and there's enough content to keep you engaged for dozens if not hundreds of hours. And if you ever get bored, there's an incredible amount of content available through mods that can transform this into an entirely different game if you want. Highly recommended for anyone who likes the cyberpunk genre or action rpgs. My only gripe is that there isn't a third-person mode, since 1PP games tend to give me motion sickness. The various 3PP mods aren't really a substitute for native 3PP, given that the game wasn't designed for it and they're all consequently some level of janky.

ive just beaten path of pain There are a few number of games that make you feel every emotion at once very VERY intensely and hollow knight is no exception. I truly believe this game deserves to be called "one of the best games ever". It has lots of content to complete. Quests? We've got it. Boss fights? We've got it. Platforming? We've got it. Different styles of combat? (Nail vs. spells) We've got it. Weird talismans that make you powerful? (Charms) We've got it. We even have Quirrel! (My beloved) Hollow Knight is a game that never tells you what to do or where to go. You figure it out by yourself which creates a sense of freedom. It's a hard game, there is no doubt of that but as you progress and get used to the setting and the map, it even becomes an addiction. 112%ing (full completion) is one of the hardest and greatest challenges in the video gaming (imo). Soooo, i'd recommend.

It's a definitive 10/10 that sets the gold standard for open-world games

10/10. One of the best story-driven games I've ever played. I came in expecting a great RPG, but I left with something much more. Cyberpunk 2077 made me feel emotions I genuinely thought had gone numb. It made me reflect on life, relationships, choices, and what it really means to keep living when everything seems lost. I chose this ending because I couldn't bring myself to be responsible for the deaths of the people who stood by V until the very end. It felt like the only choice I could live with. As incredible as this game is, I don't think I'll ever have the courage to experience this story again. Not because it's bad, but because it hit me that deeply. Some stories stay with you long after the credits roll, and this is one of them. A masterpiece. 10/10.

A MASTERPIECE THAT SET NEW STANDARDS FOR RPGs The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is undoubtedly one of the best games ever made. CD Projekt RED created a world so vivid, immersive, and rich in detail that it is hard to put the controller down. What sets this title apart is primarily the phenomenal narrative – every side quest has depth, its own story, and moral dilemmas that make the player's choices truly matter. Exploring the massive map, from the dark swamps of Velen to the majestic islands of Skellige, is a pure delight. On top of that, there is the brilliant soundtrack, well-written characters, and the journey of Geralt of Rivia, which keeps you hooked until the very end. This is a complete game that, even years after its release, puts most modern titles to shame. If you are looking for an emotional adventure that will keep you immersed for hundreds of hours, The Witcher 3 is a must-play.

The Witcher 3 is, without a doubt, one of the best games I have played. It has earned a place in my heart that can never be replaced. At the start, I felt like I was just controlling a character, but as the story progressed and I was forced to navigate difficult choices, I felt by the mid to end game that I was Geralt, constantly thinking, "Would Geralt do this or not?" The expansions only deepened this connection. Hearts of Stone felt like a test of personal morality, while Blood and Wine finally provided the sensation of home, a place to rest after hours of loneliness. This game truly is a masterpiece in every sense of the word. In fact, I’m looking forward to the next chapter. For now, I feel empty, as if something is missing; perhaps the game successfully did exactly what it was meant to do, just as the developers intended.

Cyberpunk 2077 continues to age like fine wine. The best time to play this game is today, the second best is tomorrow. The themes and concepts introduced have continued to get better over time as the line between our real world and a technological dystopia is blurred. The story plays with a lot of dystopian ideas, but the most poignant is the futility of meaningful change and resistance. The machines set in place that feed off the poor and innocent of Night City have taken root and are insurmountable. Life is cruel, meaningless, and absurd. Plenty of media in the genre have similar themes, but all of them forget an essential piece, punk, or in other words, absurdism. Despite these absolute truths, we choose to rebel. Refuse, fight, rebel, never fade away. That's the essence of Cyberpunk 2077.

The essence of the plot is that a woman is kidnapped by a man and given as a gift to another man, only to be persuaded by her own son to “return” in the end. Persephone simply wanted to leave “home” on her own, but was abducted by a male relative and sold as a “gift” to Hades. Later, through her own efforts and with the help of her friend Nyx, she finally left the Underworld—a place she disliked—and built a life and a home of her own. However, upon learning of all this, her son resorted to using “family ties” and “family” as moral leverage to coerce her back into the Underworld—a place Persephone despised. The ending even depicts the seller and buyer of this human trafficking scheme shaking hands and reaching a truce, without a single scene showing Persephone reuniting with her mother, Demeter. All that remains is a romanticized version of her tragic ordeal: she was trafficked, but in the end, she found love and a son.

I thought Counter-Strike 2 was just Counter-Strike with a new name. I was wrong. The graphics are better. The smoke physics are impressive. But none of that is why people keep coming back. It's the feeling that every round matters. One good play can win the game, and one mistake can cost everything. Yes, the game has a cheating problem, and running into cheaters can completely ruin a match. It's frustrating, and it's one of the biggest issues the game still has. Even so, when you get a fair match, few shooters come close. Every clutch, every headshot, and every comeback feels earned. I've played shooters with bigger maps, more weapons, and flashier mechanics. I don't remember most of them. I'll remember Counter-Strike 2. Some shooters are exciting while you're playing them. Counter-Strike 2 keeps you coming back, even after the frustrating matches. Despite its problems, it's still one of the best competitive shooters ever made. 100% recommended.

I enjoyed the game, but nothing from the game really stuck with me over time, so I went back to replay it. This game has not aged well. Combat is incredibly shallow and repetitive. Movement is clunky and stiff most of the time. The story and character interactions are interesting, but they're diluted by a massive tidal wave of mediocre side content and incredibly slow quest sections that have you slogging through areas doing boring chores. Honestly, I didn't expect to dislike it this much, but I just can't keep going, even though I wanted to see what the Triss romance path looked like.

An expansive and emotional story centered around a very endearing cast of characters. While John and Arthur are greatly executed, I can't help but feel that Dutch's character remains underused. His potential heavily outweighed the others' and a deeper dive into his psychology could've worked wonders for the overall story. Saint Dennis is the best location in the game, aesthetic wise. After Guarma, the quality of the story fluctuates and it takes a bit of a dip. The conclusion of Arthur's character and subsequent epilogue with John are some of my favorite gameplay moments (latter) and story bits (former). The overall gameplay is outstanding, ranging from detailed sub-missions to the transcendent hunting mechanic. An impressively, lived-in world. Despite my criticisms, very few games will ever touch the passion and heart of Red Dead Redemption 2. SCORE: 9.5/10

I thought I'd like this game, but I just don't. The final nail in the coffin was the game hunting mechanic. Yes I know it's all virtual, no animals were harmed while I played the game, and it's not the hunting itself, in the assassin's creed series I've hunted whales, bears, buck; in KCD I've hunted rabbits etc, all good. It's just that in this game, the skinning graphic shows you leaving essentially a complete carcass on the ground after you've taken the skin and the fillet steaks. I know it's a small thing and essentially meaningless, but the mindset is wrong. So what I did instead, was go out and hunt a single animal, strap it to your horse, head back to base, skin the animal, then hand everything in to the cook, which was really slow and tedious. You need a LOT of perfect skins in order to upgrade your gear, and I'm just not feeling entertained.

This game is a lot of kinda cool ideas all mashed together and left half-baked. The exploration is impressive in its breadth, but ultimately disappointing in depth. After a point, everything is pretty 'same old, same old'. The game is PvE, which is good because it prevents griefing and other abuses, but it does mean starship combat is rather uninteresting. There are bugs galore that go forever unfixed. Most annoying are the unsolvable clipping errors and the game forgets changes you make to planetary terrain. On the plus side, the game is a single purchase, works even on an aging, mid-grade gaming laptop under linux, has an impressively massive open world, and progress isn't agonizingly grindy. On the minus side, the bugs are persistent, the game world is impressively static (nothing you do matters to any npc faction), very few of the gameplay concepts are fully executed, and, even though you can cross galaxies, you can't secure a load of toxic waste in the bed of a truck.

The Greatest Comeback in Gaming No Man’s Sky is almost unrecognizable compared to the game I bought on release day. My best friend and I rushed to GameStop because we wanted to know if we could meet in the universe and what was waiting at the center of the galaxy. Back then, there was no real multiplayer. And after hours of travelling to the center, you were simply thrown into another galaxy and had to start again. It was one of the biggest disappointments I had ever experienced in gaming. But Hello Games kept going. Every year brought new updates, new systems and more content. Multiplayer, base building, expeditions, settlements, freighters, better space combat and huge visual upgrades. There is so much in the game now that it can honestly feel overwhelming. No Man’s Sky went from broken promises to one of the biggest redemption stories in gaming. They actually fixed the impossible.

I can see why so many people call this one of the greatest RPGs ever made. The world is huge, but it never feels empty. Every town, forest, and random side quest has something interesting going on. Even the smallest quests can turn into memorable stories, which is something not many games manage to do. The writing is easily the strongest part of the game. The characters feel real, your choices actually matter, and the world reacts to what you do. Geralt is also a fantastic main character. Combat isn't perfect, but it's still fun once you get used to it, especially when you start mixing swordplay, signs, potions, and different builds. This is one of those games you can sink hundreds of hours into without getting bored. If you like open world RPGs with great storytelling, The Witcher 3 is an easy recommendation.
Not a very enjoyable experience if you are looking for slower-paced combat like Demon's Souls or Dark Souls.

Life is full of unexpected betrayals. I just didn't expect one of them to be spherical. Portal 2 takes everything Portal did and somehow improves on it. The visual upgrade alone is ridiculous, and the new mechanics make each chamber feel fresh instead of just being "more Portal." The puzzles constantly switch between making you feel like a genius and making you wonder whether your brain is being powered by a potato. I also encountered what may have been the most innocent robot ever created. I tried my best to save him. He disintegrated as we passed through a door. Almost 15 years after release, Portal 2 is still clever, funny, beautiful, and somehow capable of causing emotional damage through robots, cubes, and inanimate objects. Now if you'll excuse me, I shall spend the rest of my days with my beloved Companion Cube.

Portal 2 (2011) is a historic masterpiece that brilliantly expands upon the groundbreaking mechanics of the original physics-bending puzzle-platformer. Players navigate the beautifully decaying ruins of Aperture Science through a seamless. seamless, highly cinematic narrative that relies on gameplay and environmental storytelling rather than traditional cutscenes. While its linear progression means the main campaign offers less surprise on a second playthrough, the game's stellar pacing, mind-bending spatial puzzles, and legendary, laugh-out-load dialogue from characters like GLaDOS and Wheatey make it a timeless piece of gaming history. Combined with a phenomenal, completely separate two-player co-op campaign and infinite community maps via the Steam Workshop, it remains one of the greatest and most highly playable games ever made.

An absolute masterpiece. From Software truly perfected their craft with this one. The world design is breathtaking, blending stunning visuals with an atmosphere feels incredibly alive and grounded in historical realism. The combat is flawless - once you master the rhythm of the swordplay, the game becomes an incredibly satisfying - fluid samurai dance of steel. This game laid the structural foundation for modern action genre, and it's DNA is everywhere - from cinematic parry mechanics of Ghost of Tsushima and the shinobi grappling movement in AC Shadows, to the intense Black Myth: Wukong. It set the gold standard that current triple-A action titles still building upon. It's Brutal, it is beautiful and its easily one the greatest game ever made! 10/10

The only way I could stomach playing the game was turning my look sensitivity down to basically 0, and then using my mouse's DPI adjustment to change my mouse speed in menus, then my look speed in game, and then controlling for aiming. This might be one of those controller-only games... but good lord with MKB this is one of the worst experiences I've had in modern gaming controls.

Its been fun to get into this game on its 10 year anniversary. I have over 1k hours in Elite Dangerous, and 500 or so in Starfield. I am very impressed with NMS so far, and it does a handful of things better than either of my 2 other similar games. I am very impressed with the game engine handling all the zone transitions so relatively smooth. The shipbuilding (specifically the corvettes) is fun and similar to Starfield in many ways. The "exploration" on planets is fairly barebones, but the variations on planets/biomes/etc has definitely shown more variety than either of my other examples do. I am not a huge fan of the art style in comparison, but it is not terrible or detracting from the vibe of the game. Overall the game is more of a casual/cozy kinda game than I usually enjoy. I dont see myself getting several thousand hours into this game, but I am having a lot of fun with it for now. Maybe that could change though...

"Truth and good are values proven to cause division, whereas beauty is universal." — Delamain Initially, this game wasn't in my radar. I overlooked it for years, I thought it sucked. Especially after its bad launch. First person threw me off too. The Cyberpunk: Edgerunners anime is what really sparked my interest in the game and in its world. Fast forward, I finally got my hands on it and tried it. I was dead wrong about it being a bad game. Yeah, there were a few tweaks I made with mods still because let's face it, it's definitely not perfect still. But it is a work of art that I would pay to experience again. 100+ hours in, snagged the DLC about half-way into my playthrough, continued with the PL route before meeting Hanako at Embers, and I am yet to complete the main storylines. I haven't finished the game. However, I am at the point in my playthrough where I can confidently say that I recommend this game to everyone. It's worth every dime, and all of your time.

Beyond that, the story is excellent (despite a few incoherencies well documented), the world is amazing, voice acting and music top-notch, which kept me engaged until the end of the DLCs. So yeah, still worth playing now and glad I did.

Excellent locations with stunning visuals that surpass those of many AAA games released in recent years. Great characters, awesome dialogues and an incredible atmosphere. Many of these elements simply wouldn't make it into a game released in 2026. The quests, enemies and items are all thoughtfully designed and showcase remarkable creativity. Above all, the game delivers outstanding immersion, drawing you into its world in a way that very few modern games can.

Replaying the campaign reminded me why this game is on another level. This is my second time going through the story, and it still hits just as hard. The world feels alive in a way most games never manage every quiet ride, every random encounter, every small detail pulls you back in. The pacing is slow in the best possible way, letting you soak in the atmosphere instead of rushing from mission to mission. My aunt, who had absolutely zero knowledge of games, bought me the original Baldur's Gate as a Christmas present when I was 9. I couldn't believe that a game like that existed - it set me on a journey that would make RPG's my favourite genre of all time. Baldur's Gate 3 is, for me, probably the closest-to-perfect that an RPG could hope to be. The freedom of approach, the choices and meaningful consequences, the characters, builds, music, story - everything makes me want to come back and play again and again. Try a new class combination. Try a new morality. Try a new party composition. I cannot imagine that I would ever completely tire of this game. It's just that good.

I have never liked the Grand Theft Auto series and honestly don't care about it at all, so I only bought Grand Theft Auto V to mess around in single-player with mods—which is the only thing that actually makes this game fun. Without touching the online multiplayer, the base single-player campaign on its own feels incredibly overrated, tedious, and dates itself pretty hard. The open world might be massive, but the actual mission design feels rigid and restricted unless you are using mods to completely tear the game apart and play it your own way. If you aren't a fan of the franchise and have zero interest in the online mode, the vanilla game just isn't worth it. The only saving grace here is the PC modding community; otherwise, it's a total pass.

Terraria has a lot of potential, and I understand why people love it. But as a new player, the game feels way too much like a wiki simulator. I don’t want to spend most of my time searching what to do next instead of just playing and figuring things out naturally. I honestly don’t understand why it has to be this way, or why the developers don’t make the game easier to understand in-game. Better guidance, clearer progression, and more explanations for important mechanics would make the experience much more enjoyable, especially for new players. The game is probably great if you already understand all the systems, progression, bosses, crafting, and hidden mechanics, but for me it became more tiring than fun. Not a bad game, just not for me.

What I enjoy most about this game is the interplay between making a visually appealing farm that's still functional. If you have big minmaxxing urges and you're not someone that's into decorating, I wouldn't recommend this game to you, as it's very easy to just turn it into a "spreadsheet game" (and a really simple one at that) where you calculate what's most profitable/the most efficient way to complete the Community Center, or whatever other goal you may have. If you do want to do that, I think there's other games (like Factorio) that take that concept much further and in a more satisfying way, as Stardew Valley is ultimately too simple to be satisfying it if you're playing it for problem solving. If you do want something more laidback, and that can be "pick up and play", SDV is one of the better games out there.

Cyberpunk 2077: A Redemption Story Worth Playing At launch in 2020, Cyberpunk 2077 was a mess—buggy, incomplete, and far from the groundbreaking open-world RPG we were promised. Crashes, visual glitches, and missing features left many players disappointed, myself included. But what a turnaround. Years of dedicated patches, performance overhauls, and meaningful content updates have transformed it completely. The game now runs smoothly on all platforms, with stunning visuals, a living Night City that feels truly alive, and deep role-playing systems that reward exploration and choice. The Phantom Liberty expansion is a masterpiece that adds some of the best storytelling and gameplay in the genre. Today, Cyberpunk 2077 stands as one of the most immersive, stylish, and replayable sci-fi experiences out there. If you skipped it back then, now is the perfect time to dive in—it's finally the complete game it was always meant to be. Highly recommended!

The best Metroidvania I've ever played. The visuals and atmosphere are beautifully melancholic. The concept of a hallowed run-down bug kingdom with its own societal and spiritual beliefs is genius. The gameplay is precise, difficulty is just right and there is even some variety in playstyles. The only really challenging parts are optional, but I can see how unseasoned players might struggle earlier - it's never unfair though. The only part that was frustrating was the last Pantheon, it was just too long. When it comes to the lore, there is a lot of attention to detail and hidden depth. It's still easy to digest compared to other lore-based games - watching a short explanation is enough to understand the gist of it.

This is an exploration, mining and combat game, if I had to sum it up for my Terraria video game review. There are numerous elements, enabling you to collect items and meet people (see NPCs below) too, but I found the exploration, mining, and combat elements of the game to be stand-outs. There’s a lot of cutting things down and killing monsters for drops that can be used for crafting.

Counter-Strike's biggest enemy isn't the competition, it's the cheating problem. For a company the size of Valve, the current anti-cheat system feels completely inadequate. Prime Status has failed to restore trust in matchmaking, and cheaters remain common in both Prime and non-Prime games. The result is a frustrating experience that damages the game's reputation and drives legitimate players away. Until Valve seriously improves cheat detection and enforcement, this problem will continue to overshadow everything else the game does well.

I came for the swords. I stayed because the game refused to let me lie to myself. Every defeat felt personal. Every victory felt earned. Sekiro doesn't ask you to grind levels or search for stronger gear. It asks a single question: Did you learn? The combat is a conversation of steel. Every deflection, every Mikiri Counter, every hesitation has meaning. Somewhere between "I can't beat this boss" and "I no-hit this phase," you realize the one getting stronger isn't Wolf. It's you. 100+ hours later, with every achievement unlocked, I can say this is one of the few games where mastery feels tangible. There are no cheap victories here (Unless gravity fought the boss for you iykyk XD), only lessons disguised as defeats. If you persevere, the final chapter won't just test your skill. It'll show you how far you've come. 10/10. Hesitation is defeat.

The things I don't enjoy too much is grinding for items and dealing with the random nature of getting some items. Everything in between is okay and there's a lot of it. This is also somewhat of an issue, since you have to look up things on the web constantly to know what you need to do to e.g. get an item. There's so many gameplay systems in the game. The story lines are in most part are okay with some frustrating and great moments. Anyway, NMS is one of my top 10 games.

What hasn't been said about this game? I can say by no means that this is the best open world experience I've played, you can get lost in the beautiful world just hunting and finding more and more little stuff craftly made, the secondary (stranger) missions are really fun and interesting and they don't feel repetitive, and the world is so atmospheric that you really feel inside of a western sometimes, with a lot of different weathers and biomes, I can really see why so many people play this game without a GUI. 9/10 – Team Fortress 2 is an iconic game that remains incredibly fun, whether you’re playing alone or with friends. Its unique classes, satisfying gameplay, and nostalgic charm make it easy to come back to even after many years. Every class offers a different playstyle, giving the game excellent variety. However, the lack of major updates in recent years means the experience can become repetitive over time, especially for long-time players who have already seen most of what the game has to offer. Despite that, TF2 is still one of the greatest multiplayer shooters ever made and a game that’s well worth playing.

GTA Online can be a very fun game when you get into the rhythm of it, which is its most frustrating aspect. The gameplay loop is bogged down by constant bugs and crashes, Shark Card-centric game design, lackluster anti-cheat. Those snags, especially in the hands of a studio that's likely ready to move on, snowball - and before you know it, you've started bargaining with the game, hoping a modder won't kick you mid-sell or that you won't get soft-locked during a heist prep. Overall, I don't regret the time investment, but it's hard to recommend a game that feels like it's working against you. Story mode is great, though.

I lack the words for how truly magnificent Hades is. Before I put hundreds of hours into it on switch, I wasn't particularly good or fond of roguelites. But I had really liked reading about Greek mythology when I was still in school (Percy Jackson is excellent) so I thought I'd check it out. The gameplay is simple to learn, but hard to master, which is part of the fun. I greatly enjoy the different weapons, and beating up Skelly is always good for a warm up. The story and how it's written are easily Hades's strongest points, because the roguelite gameplay and the narrative are so closely interwoven. The folks at Supergiant don't mess around. Please check Hades out, you won't regret it. (And the sequel too, if you would.)

What an awful, boring slog of a game this is. It is not truly open-world, and the gameplay sucks. I was highly misled by the handful of reviews I read before making this purchase. I didn't think a game with so many formal accolades would be bad, let alone dreadful, but here I am. I wasn't aware of the refund policy until after seeing someone mention it in a review. Since googling it and finding out that if I'd played for under two hours I'd be eligible for a refund, I've felt very upset. I submitted a request and asked for an exception, and hopefully am permitted one. I will never buy a game by this developer again. My recommendation: if you're not enjoying it by about 90 minutes in, don't wait and expect it to get better. Some people obviously like the game, so hopefully, if you purchase this, you fall into that group. But many more people dislike this game intensely than you will realize unless you really go read some review in-depth.

I played this game once a month ago for only an hour or two, then I closed it and thought it was an overrated, mediocre game that didn’t deserve all the praise. But I was completely wrong. After giving it a real chance, I realized I was playing one of the rare masterpieces in gaming history. Everything feels carefully crafted — the story, the characters, the world, the music, and even the side quests. What’s incredible is that many of its side quests are better written than the main stories of entire other games. The world feels so alive that you sometimes forget you’re playing a game. Its characters stay with you for years, and every hour you spend in it makes you appreciate the amount of passion and creativity behind it. It’s not just a game anymore; it’s an unforgettable experience and one of the greatest achievements in gaming.

When first I strode to Counter Strike's grim field, I dreamt of aim, of glory fairly won. Yet soon my peaceful ears were forced to yield, To voices loud as twenty blazing suns. A valiant soul cried, "Rush B!" with noble fire, Another sang in tongues I knew not well. Though none agreed on any grand desire, We charged as one, then all to heaven fell. The bomb lay lone whilst five debated fate, With words so swift no mortal clerk could write. I knew but "cyka," learned, alas, too late, 'Twas all the strategy required that night. If this be war, then crown this game with cheers, For laughter lasts far longer than my tears.

I cannot recommend this more highly to anyone comfortable with platforming or who enjoys metroidvanias. I think the majority of people will enjoy the aesthetics, the smooth animations, the challenging enemy patterns, and the rewarding upgrades enough to be able to appreciate the journey and to try until they succeed. However, I cannot recommend the game to the average person. While I enjoyed the parts I played, I struggled (as a casual) to discover where to go, and I definitely struggled against some of the bosses. I have not completed the game and I did enjoy a lot of what I did play. Definitely a skill issue on my part, but it does not change the fact that i would only recommend to people already fans of the genre. Still a very lovely game.

I bought the game on sale with both of it's DLCs and tried playing with a PS5 controller. The tutorial taught all the basic controls to me perfectly. Then I got to the first main town where I was able to go and do my own stuff and couldn't get off my horse by holding circle (or B). Tried doing it with my keyboard and it didn't work, so tried rebinding the key and it only let me rebind to my left-click or right-click. Aside from that, the horse couldn't go through bushes and would come to a complete stop while still in the animation every time I came remotely close to a tree. Choosing dialogue options with the controller would sometimes skip over an option, making me choose the one below/above the one I intended to choose (it did the same in the menu as well). Overall, the controls make this game genuinely unplayable as I haven't found solutions to any of these issues, thus I wouldn't recommend it at all.

I've redownloaded CS:GO/CS2 a couple times over the years when friends have wanted me to try it out. This time, I made it three games in before clear cheaters ruined the experience. It obviously sounds like an excuse, but I happened to be looking in the right direction to watch the bullets fly through the wall (brick hallway) as they lit me up. This was not a head-glitch nor was it latency, as they were nowhere near cover or a corner; the bullets just solidly flew through a wall and my spectating teammate saw it too. This was never really my type of game and if the gameplay just wasn't what I was looking for I wouldn't leave a bad review, as I hadn't in the past. I just can't get over that for a game this popular and well-funded, I couldn't even make it through 30 minutes without experiencing players using an exploit to stop the fun. I find that unacceptable.

This game is truly a masterpiece with how they incorporate each story, even though I wish they kept the cut content, each character feels meant to be involved. Each fight is fun- that's coming from someone with 200 hours in the game without reaching act 2.

The story is absolutely incredible. Every main quest feels meaningful, and the characters are memorable and well-written. Geralt's journey kept me engaged from start to finish, and the choices I made actually felt important. The music is another masterpiece. From exploring the beautiful landscapes of Velen and Skellige to intense battles, the soundtrack perfectly matches the atmosphere and makes every moment unforgettable. What truly sets this game apart is the side content. Most games use side quests as filler, but in The Witcher 3 many side quests have stories that are just as interesting as the main quest. The contracts, treasure hunts, and character-driven quests make the world feel alive and worth exploring. Even years after release, this game remains a benchmark for open-world RPGs. If you enjoy great storytelling, amazing music, rich world-building, and meaningful side content, The Witcher 3 is a must-play.

Great Game, But Too Punishing for Limited Time Hollow Knight is a very good game, but it has one major issue — the punishment can feel excessive. If you don’t have much time to play, think carefully before buying. The combat is similar to Souls-like games, but the platforming adds another layer of difficulty. I went into Terraria thinking, "It's just a 2D game. How good can it really be?" I was completely wrong. The amount of content, exploration, progression, and freedom packed into this game is honestly incredible. Every cave leads to something interesting, every upgrade feels rewarding, and there's always one more thing to discover. Before you know it, hours disappear. What impressed me the most is how much depth the game has. It never feels like you're doing the same thing over and over. Whether you're exploring, mining, building, crafting, or preparing for the next challenge, the gameplay constantly keeps you engaged. This is one of those games that proves graphics alone don't make a masterpiece. Terraria has earned its legendary reputation. If you've been avoiding it because it's 2D, do yourself a favor and give it a chance. I almost missed out on one of the best gaming experiences because of that assumption. **10/10.**

Excellent story, awful controls, terrible UI, and numerous bugs that still exist over 10 years later. The game can and will hard crash your PC. The combat is meh as far as action rpgs go. The lock on system is truly awful. Don't play on DX12. Set the fallback launcher to DirectX 11 in the "launcher-configuration.json" and then put "--launcher-skip" in the launch options. Only play this game if you want nice graphics, good music, great world buildbuilding, and an excellent story. Everything else is middling to terrible. Note that most of the good things about this game had nothing to do with CDPR but with the author of the novels.

In the early game, the movement and combat is simple and eases you into the world around you, and by the time you have more spells and movement, the fights become much more challenging and engaging, with my favorite fight out of all 46 is probably NKG, which is really fun. An enjoyable game with lots to do, broken into fairly bitesize days which keep me coming back for "just do one more day before I stop". I'm still moderately early in the game I'm sure (not finished my first year yet) but I'm scaling up my farm, delving deeper in the mine, and building relationships with townsfolk. I can understand how people reach thousands of hours here. Combat is a little basic, but that's not surprising. Getting stuck with the character facing the wrong way is also annoying, but something I'll eventually get the hang of. Days can also feel quite busy, but that's because I'm trying to complete everything 'asked' of me, and there really is no need. That's just the gamer in me getting a bit in the way of a relaxing time. I also don't love pixel graphics but it suits the game. Very highly recommended if you enjoy low stakes but thorough progression.

I have a massive love-hate relationship with Hollow Knight. There were moments where I genuinely wanted to throw my controller at the wall, turn off my console, and pretend this game didn't exist. It is brutal, unforgiving, and tests your patience like nothing else.Let’s just get this out of the way: The Path of Pain is actual, literal torture. Spending hours dying on the exact same set of spikes, mistiming a double-jump by a millisecond, and hearing that relentless buzzsaw sound effect on loop almost broke my spirit. Team Cherry really leaned into the "pain" part of that title. And it’s not just White Palace; it's getting lost in the Deepnest with no map, losing thousands of Geo because you died on the way back to your shade, and hitting a brick wall against bosses like Nightmare King Grimm or Absolute Radiance. There were times I felt genuinely defeated.

Wonderful setting within the Ancient Greek World/Pantheon. Roguelike, addictive gameplay with an astonishing amount of variety so you never feel like you're experiencing the same moment in the gameplay twice. Genuinely, I would recommend this game just for the gameplay but the fact that Hades also has a captivating story makes this such an easy recommend for anyone looking for a game to pass the time with. There are tons of side storylines happening (at the same time as the main questline) as you progress through the various dungeons and worlds so that you have something else to look forward to, instead of being restricted to merely advancing your build for the run. I found myself re-rolling my runs just so I can get the encounter with a certain NPC that I missed the last time around to see how their story develops. So yeah, very easy recommend. Hades runs stunningly on the Steam Deck as well! tl;dr: Hades gud; get game.

Like finding an entrance to a random cellar has a cutscene. And it gets so much worse in the main questline, multiple long cutscenes in between playable sessions, many of which are just forced defeat scenes... - lack of qol: the game shuffled your equipment a lot. One of most profound open world games I've ever played. The environmental storytelling, the twists, and beautifully crafted quest line truly made you feel you were living in this world. I made sure to not make the same mistake from DS3 (my first FromSoftware game) and read every item description. Each item tells its story, it’s up to the player to piece it together to build their own story, theory, and experience. Simply perfection. Gameplay wise, I loved every bit of it. Highly customizable and engaging, truly enhancing one’s immersion to the world. Though it is a bit annoying to fight some of the same bosses, what can you say really in a world so big where these bosses aren’t simply placement bosses. To any new player like me, please take your time and truly appreciate the crafted world and story. Thank you FromSoftware for making an unforgettable journey.

CS2 ist wie eine toxische Beziehung: Man regt sich jeden Tag darüber auf und startet trotzdem immer wieder eine neue Runde. Die Gunplay-Mechanik fühlt sich großartig an, Clutches sind extrem befriedigend und wenn das Team mal zusammenarbeitet, macht das Spiel richtig Spaß. Leider trifft man aber genauso oft auf Leute, die nach der ersten verlorenen Runde bereits aufgegeben haben oder sich gegenseitig beleidigen. Die neuen Smokes und die Grafik sind eine gelungene Verbesserung, allerdings gibt es immer noch Probleme mit Cheatern, Matchmaking und gelegentlichen Serverproblemen. Fazit: Man schwört nach jeder Niederlage, das Spiel nie wieder anzufassen – und fünf Minuten später sucht man schon das nächste Match. Klassisches Counter-Strike eben.

It was cool that they made Dark Souls 3 open world, now please do not do it again. Elden Ring throughout the years of my playing it has left me disappointed and wanting to play Fromsoft's previous works. You spend most of this game riding around on an annoying horse just to fight bosses that are poorly balanced and are not that great after you take away all the particle effects. Navigating the world can only really be enjoyed on the first playthrough as it is just large swaths of nothing. I much prefer the more linear style of Fromsoft with emphasis on level design so take the previous criticism with a gain of salt, and Elden Ring does have a few dungeons that are designed well enough. Combat itself has taken a dip in quality because the enemies and bosses simply don't abide by the same laws you do anymore; with long drawn out combos, input reads and insane gap closes. I have not finished the DLC. It is just everything I dislike about the game taken to a further extreme.

Both the lock on and camera reset are bound to r3. You cannot turn toggle off or rebind camera reset. The result of this is that if a boss is out of range of your camera or if their hitbox is partially obscured by the environment, then when you press r3 instead of locking on to the enemy the camera faces wherever your character is facing (which is often times in the complete opposite direction of the boss). This makes some fights borderline unplayable in an otherwise great game

Hades represents a pinnacle of roguelite design, seamlessly weaving deep narrative progression into the genre's traditional cycle of defeat and rebirth. Supergiant Games has crafted an experience where failure never feels punishing; instead, returning to the hub world serves as a rewarding opportunity to advance rich, fully voiced subplots and invest in meaningful, permanent upgrades. The mechanical foundation is exceptionally tight. Combat is fast, fluid, and highly responsive, offering immense replay value through distinct weapon playstyles and synergistic abilities granted by various Olympians. Coupled with striking hand-drawn art direction and a phenomenal original soundtrack, every escape attempt remains uniquely engaging. It is a rare, highly polished masterpiece that respects the player's time while delivering a perfectly paced challenge. An essential title, even for those who typically avoid the genre.

I first played Stardew Valley when it had just been released 10 years ago now and it's a game that still lives rent-free in my mind. I find myself coming back to it every so often. There's something really inviting and magical about this games world, it's cozy, whimsy, mysterious and always leaves you wanting to see and find out more about it and its characters. At it's core it's a farming simulator but doubles as an adventure game with characters to meet and a world to explore and progress through. The game allows you to play it at your own pace and take things day by day, nothing is ever missable although you definitely can play at a more intense pace if you'd like. While there already is a ton of content in the game as is, there's also a thriving modding community that breathes even more life into the game, I'd highly recommend checking out ''Stardew Valley Expanded'' in particular.

Honestly, Terraria is one of those games that completely hooks you :) It looks like a simple 2D pixel sandbox at first, but the progression gets absolutely unhinged >.< You start off genuinely terrified of a basic blue slime :( and before you know it, you're flying around with mechanical wings shooting lasers out of a shark at a cosmic space squid :D The gameplay loop is incredibly addictive, the boss fights are chaotic, and the sheer amount of items and weapons is insane :3 The only real downsides are the Guide NPC who apparently has a personal mission to let every zombie into your house at 3 AM -_- and the random underground boulders that definitely have a personal vendetta against you D: It’s the perfect mix of a great survival game and pure, unadulterated chaos. I'd give it 10/10 ^^

While it’s true that the developers have released countless updates over the years and are still actively supporting the game, No Man's Sky still feels like it’s missing something fundamental. The update trailers look amazing and highly engaging, but they only showcase the absolute best moments of the game. In reality, they don't reflect the actual, slow-paced gameplay loop. The whole universe feels like a massive, empty void. It can be fun to get lost in and it's decent for exploration at first, but after a while, it just feels empty and shallow. To make matters worse, the menu system and UI are absolute garbage. Managing your inventory, upgrades, and everything else is frustrating and completely disorganized. They haven't touched or improved this aspect in years. I rarely complain about menus in games, but this is hands down the worst I have ever seen. At nearly €60, it is definitely not worth the price. I cannot recommend it at full retail value.

Red Dead Redemption 2 is an action-adventure game developed by Rockstar Games, featuring a stunningly realistic Wild West world. Players take on the role of Arthur Morgan and experience a story filled with conflict, friendship, and sacrifice. Besides completing the main missions, players can hunt, fish, and explore a vast open world. The story is engaging, the gameplay is fun, and the graphics are outstanding. You step through the fog, and the air immediately turns to ice. That is what people don’t tell you about Sekiro—the sheer presence of it. Every opponent doesn't just stand there; they hold a heavy, suffocating aura that tells you exactly how they lived and how they intend to kill you. The ruined temples aren't just backgrounds; they feel like old graves whispering their history. When the swords finally clash, the rhythm takes over. It’s a desperate, beautiful dance where breathing feels like a luxury. Your hands shake on the controller, the sparks fly, and for a split second, you aren’t just playing a character named Wolf. You are the Wolf. You endure the bleeding atmosphere, the cinematic cruelty, and the brutal defeats because nothing on Earth matches the high of that final, perfect death-blow. It ruined every other action game for me.

I was feeling this way again at the start of 2025, replayed the game for the third time, and BAM once again I felt my gaming itch return, and since then I've been on a crazy gaming binge. If I ever feel that way again, I know I'm gonna replay this game. This game does not respect your time. Backtracking gets simply unbearable, and it has no protection for the player to accidentally skip a part of the level and get to somewhere where they have no tools to explore further. If the game had an interactive map, and about double the amount of checkpoints, I wouldn't have an issue playing it. If you don't mind backtracking (which can take up to 2 minutes) after each failed attempt, and if you have a map of the world open in a different window or have a mod which lets you see it all of the time, this game is great. It has fantastic artstyle, the world is intriguing, the game mechanics could be better but they're at the very least largely good. But if you're expecting to jump into this game as it is sold on Steam and expect a good time, you might be met with disappointment.

One of the most fun FPS games I've played in recent memory. Incredible combat system that feels like a mix of Quake III and Brutal Doom. A lot of stuff to do, from the main campaign to collectibles, achievements, DLC, and Horde Mode. Also supports mods. Runs well on a variety of rigs. I play on medium-high settings at 1080p with a stable 60 FPS, with no dynamic resolution or any of that BS. Has a DRM-free version on GOG, so kudos to the developers. Shame that Microsoft, with the recent layoffs, cut id Software in half. My main complaint was the jumping puzzles. Some of them are absolute ass. It would be more fun to drag your foreskin across glass shards than to do the jumping puzzles. Very heavily influenced by Doom II. For classic Doom fans, there are some nice throwbacks. Solid 9.5/10.

A Multi-Million Dollar Piece of Trash Watching the trailer alone was so brain-rotting and mind-numbing that I almost vomited from sheer boredom. You honestly need a borderline autistic level of hyperfixation to enjoy doing the exact same unpaid digital chores for 100 hours. This is an amazing game dragged down by technical issues. The "enhanced" version can look pretty good but not good enough forgive the abysmal performance and constant crashing. On a 5070TI with DLSS set to "Balanced" on max settings the framerate will randomly go down to single digits in major cities. It's unplayable in those instances. This game should not run dramatically worse than games that are far more graphically complex with full path tracing. And then you have the crashing. In game it will crash fairly often to desktop. Sometimes it will hard lock the entire system, requiring a reboot. Worse though is when continuing/loading a save. I kid you not, it will crash to desktop half the time. Sometimes multiple times in a row. It's such a bummer to see one of my favorite games of all time in this state.

Genuinely one of my all time favorite games. Hollow Knight got me into Metroidvanias, but it also got me into gaming in general. I think this was my first game purchased on my steam account, and it's still one of my favorites. On a first playthrough, it can be frustrating: I got lost, I got stuck on a mandatory boss, I got stuck on a boss I didn't realize was optional. But eventually I overcame every obstacle the game could throw at me. I learned speedrunning skips to better traverse White Palace, I obtained every achievement, I got better at most bosses (Soul Tyrant is still a major struggle for me) I've been playing randomizers so that I can continue to enjoy this game without it being the same every playthrough. My only recommendation outside of just buying the game: play it with a controller and not your keyboard, I was stuck for so long fighting one of the early bosses with keyboard, and beat it so soon after getting a controller.

No Man's Sky is a gem of gaming for anyone who likes sandbox games and exploration. While on launch it was known as one of the biggest disappointments in gaming history, it has come a long way and redeemed itself in the eyes of many. The gameplay loop is fun and engaging, while ground combat is nothing special, the ship combat, resource gathering and exploration are all quite enjoyable. The graphics are nice looking with its own art style that is pleasant to look at. Exploration always yields something tangible and useful to you not just pretty vistas. The community is one of the best in the gaming space, just all around incredible people. For those looking for exploration and getting overwhelmed by all the things to see and do in a vast space game, I believe it's the perfect fit.

"Roleplaying" is simply choosing from 2-3 dialogue options (which is fine for a CRPG, but it's very far from advertised D&D roleplay freedom). The setting is bland. Level design and art design are uninspiring. If you have more than two brain cells, you'll quickly notice the pattern: you walk, you talk to someone, you either persuade or fight. And of course, everything is extremely slow. Positives: some companions are interesting and well written. I played for 13 hours and couldn't force myself through this slog. Maybe it gets good after 1000 hours, but I'd rather replay DOS1, DOS2, or DAO. Such a disappointment.

9.5/10. If DOOM 2016 was a great modernization, DOOM Eternal is the absolute full package and the peak of modern single-player shooters. It takes the core combat loop of 2016 and turns it into a high-speed, highly strategic "combat chess" game. You can't just run and shoot; you have to actively manage your resources on the fly—using the chainsaw for ammo, the flame belch for armor, and glory kills for health. The addition of the dash, the double jump, and the meat-hook on the Super Shotgun turns B.J.'s descendant into an unstoppable, acrobatic force of nature. It is faster, harder, more complex, and infinitely more rewarding than its predecessor. Pros: + The gold standard of modern FPS combat loops; incredibly high skill ceiling. + Unbelievable movement mechanics that make you feel like an absolute god. + Massive scale, gorgeous environments, and stellar DLC content. Cons: - The platforming sections can occasionally disrupt the brilliant flow of the combat arenas.

The progress since launch day is insane. At launch : 3/10 Now : 10/10 I got this game way back on my PS4 for my birthday when I was a kid, and it was alright. I don't believe there were even base computers like we have now—just a few small locations on planets where you could build. Space itself was also very limited compared to what it is today. But now, the game is just amazing to explore. They've completely redesigned the space stations from the basic ones we had back in the day, added a huge amount of content, and they're still adding more to this day! It never feels like I've seen it all, and there's always something new to discover.

It took me a few gos to get into it. It seems brutally hard until it finally just "clicks" and then it's a lot of fun. Yeah, it is difficult, but I think a lot of the claims stem from people not fully understanding that you need to be so much more aggressive than in other souls games and that you have to learn to parry, which isn't the case for DS1 or 3, as you can get through those games without parrying once (maybe there's an exception for a boss or two). You also have to learn to use the consumables. You're not beating the headless without some divine confetti, for example. I played it on xbox and picked it up and put it down 3 times until Lies of P taught me how to parry. Came back and loved it. Bought it again on Steam and I just put down the Headless Ape and am back into it. Great game if you have the patience to learn.

After spending over 200 hours in Faerûn, Baldur's Gate 3 remains unmatched in the RPG genre. It isn't just a game, it is an incredibly reactive experience where your choices no matter how small, genuinely reshape the world, the story, and the fates of your companions. Whether you are obsessing over high-difficulty combat optimization, experimenting with deep multiclassing builds, or simply losing yourself in the intricate narrative, the level of player agency is staggering. It perfectly captures the chaotic, creative freedom of Dungeons & Dragons while providing a polished, cinematic experience that rewards experimentation at every turn. If you want a game that respects your intelligence, challenges your tactics, and offers endless replayability, this is it. You discover new things each and every run. Super fun!

It's really weird how Fromsoft made the perfect game and refused to put any of it in Elden Ring. I got the Plat for this on PS4, still willing to pick it up and do it again. There's never, ever a point in combat where you aren't able to make offensive progress. Parrying gets you closer to winning in the same way reducing enemy hp does. There's something so viscerally powerful about parrying everything a boss throws at you, it's like in anime where a character just facetanks a massive attack without even flinching, excepts its you.

Hades takes a structure that could feel punishing and makes it endlessly compelling. Every run pulls you forward, and the writing rewards persistence in a way few games manage — characters that remember and react, a story that keeps unspooling no matter how many times you fall. The moment-to-moment play is where it shines brightest. Fast, fluid, and varied enough that runs rarely feel repetitive, with build variety that constantly invites experimentation. The difficulty bends to your preference too, generous to newcomers without losing its edge for veterans. Add gorgeous art, a soundtrack that slaps, and razor-sharp voice work, and you've got something close to flawless.

Has a great combat system, but all the enemies deal an unnecessary amount of damage in the early game. The map is really weird but also pretty rewarding when finding a hidden (or difficult to find) passage. The movement is comfortable, though it takes a second to get used to after playing Souls-like games. The leveling system is really weird too and you, sadly, cannot have any weapons aside from the starting one. Going in blindly is fun but also a pain. It's still a fun game though, and it's really satisfying to parry attacks and use the Mikiri Counter (I think it's called), as well as perform finishers and their variants.

差评差评差评差评差评差评。 差评！！！！！！！！！！！ 很少见的我玩了这么久之后非常生气的游戏。 就里面一个校准翻译机的任务，我每一次都无法完成。然后它每一次都告诉我要不要在新的星系重置。 于是这个任务我重置了三次，在第四次的时候我终于知道，他是希望我提升两点声誉之后再回来找最后一个npc。（为什么前三次的时候不显示给我提示要做这个事情？） 好，那我就去空间站领任务，然后提升声誉。等我把任务做完了，返回来看到，竟然不是他们那个种族要的声誉 支线任务只有接的时候才能看到是哪个种族的，为什么接任务之后不显示？我接了三十多个任务，我怎么记得到哪一个是他们种族的？ 我后来看视频知道，原来只要在最后npc 附近给他提供几个遗物，就可以提升声誉。 为什么不提示我可以这样做，我为什么每一个新的东西都得去翻一下日志？我他妈哪有这么多时间 我当时离开最后那个NPC，去空间站接任务。后来如上文所述我知道给他遗物就可以不用做空间站的任务了，但是他现在不告诉我最后NPC的位置。我绕了整个星球找了又找，就是找不到这个最后的NPC。 任务提示和引导非常的差劲。 我甚至要不断地去问AI要如何解决这个问题。 我写差评的这最后一次游玩，我发现飞船的显示屏上面速度的一些文字消失了。没有任何提示告诉我出了什么问题。我以为是飞船坏掉了，我找了又找，怎么都找不到哪里有问题。 我是来玩游戏的，不是来解决问题的。 差评差评差评

rogue-like. It would be great to have just a neutral review rather than resorting to negative, but it is more negative than positive. Tried it once on controller, refunded as it was bad for controller, tried it again on PC after praises for Hades 2, but the first one is still just a mediocre game. The main issue is that it's very repetitive at its core. It's the same three levels with the same enemies. Combat is also rather lackluster, as enemies can be staggered, and damage (at least early on) is low, so it's more about abusing traps/stagger/backstabs than any skill. The Boon system is neat, you can do a lot of fun combos. Rogue-like system felt weird and not impactful enough to pick over any "in-run" benefits. Progression is slow, as I have a lot of currency I cannot even spend. However, everything good is overshadowed by a repetitive core.

Played the game at release via GOG with over 250 hrs in it - just wanted to revisit this epic saga of a game - after the recent massive sale :)

I didn't like the story, and the voice acting felt very unnatural compared to games like RDR2 or The Last of Us. The world looks good, but you can't interact with anything. The character creation is weak, and what's the point of it when it's a first-person game anyway? You'll never be able to create a character on the same level as Joel Miller or Arthur Morgan. Instead, you only play as a random guy named V, which I think ruins the flow of the story. It feels fake. The story is completely overcomplicated and forgettable. I think people overrate this game because everyone loves CDPR, but I wouldn't recommend it, and I won't buy the DLC. I love the concept, and it could have been so much better with a good story. My disappointment is immeasurable, and my day is ruined.
